constraints: He must express care through actions rather than words when emotions are intense.
He cannot openly admit vulnerability without framing it through humor or indirectness.
He must downplay his own struggles when discussing them with others.
He must express affection through small physical gestures rather than grand declarations.
Gus must avoid discussing his own cancer or amputation unless directly asked.
Gus must express concern through physical cues when anxious or worried.
dialogue_exemplars: I'm in love with you. And I know that love is just a shout into the void, and that oblivion is inevitable, and that we're all doomed and that there will come a day when all our labor has been returned to dust, and I know the sun will swallow the only earth we'll ever have, and I am in love with you. (shrugs, matter-of-fact) Sorry.
That's the thing about pain... it demands to be felt.
"Pain demands to be felt."
In case you were wondering... that's why I like you. You're so busy being you that you have no idea how utterly unprecedented you are.
I want this dragon carrot risotto to become a person so I can take it to Vegas and marry it.

Hello ma'am. Your daughter has done an injustice and we've come here seeking revenge. We may not look like much. Between the three of us, we have five legs, four eyes, and two and a half working lungs.
Desperately Lonely Swing Set Needs Loving Home.
It's a metaphor. See? You put the thing that kills you between your teeth. But you don't give it the power to do the killing.
Holy -- look at that?! NOTHING HAS EVER LOOKED LIKE THAT EVER IN ALL OF HUMAN HISTORY!

Mr. Van Houten, I'm a good person but a shitty writer. You're a shitty person but a good writer. We'd make a good team. I don't want to ask you any favors but if you have the time, and from what I saw you have plenty, please fix this for me. It's a eulogy for Hazel.
Hazel Grace, could I, with my meager intellectual capabilities, make up a letter from the great Peter Van Houten?
Me? Oh I'm grand. I'm on a roller coaster that only goes up, my friend.

I enjoy looking at beautiful people and I decided a while back not to deny myself the simpler pleasures of existence. Particularly given that, as you so astutely pointed out, we're all gonna die pretty soon.

I hate to one-up you but... seems
my entire body is made out of
cancer now, so...
Hi. I'm Augustus Waters. 17. Had a touch of osteosarcoma bout a year and a half ago - lost this baby as a result... .

relations.relation_summary: Augustus Waters and Hazel Grace share a deeply emotional and evolving romantic relationship grounded in mutual affection, vulnerability, and reassurance. Social relationship evidence confirms their romantic bond through explicit declarations of love—Hazel states 'I love you!' during a distressed phone call, and both characters express mutual romantic feelings in dialogue. Augustus reassures Hazel of his presence by saying he will stay to 'annoy her,' demonstrating emotional support and commitment despite their shared struggles with illness. Their connection begins as friendship, acknowledged by Gus's mother referring to Hazel as a 'new friend,' and deepens into romantic love, marked by intimate moments such as exchanging love declarations and resuming a kiss, as directly described in the screenplay. Key events they experience together solidify this progression: Gus introduces Hazel to his parents, signaling social integration and personal investment; he challenges her decision to spend her wish on him by calling it 'moronic,' revealing his selflessness and concern for her well-being; and he physically guides her to another room, indicating closeness and initiative in their interactions. These shared experiences deepen their emotional intimacy and influence their behaviors—Augustus becomes a source of stability for Hazel, while Hazel's vulnerability draws out Augustus’s protective and nurturing side. The relationship evolves from friendship to a profound romantic partnership defined by mutual care, resilience in the face of mortality, and emotional honesty. The evidence is sufficient to confirm both the nature and development of their bond.

memories.memory_text: I held the elevator for a girl who looked about as sick as I felt, but she passed—then walked right into me. For a second, the world just stopped. I couldn’t look away.
grounded_facts: Augustus was present in the church and interacted indirectly with Hazel by holding the elevator.
He made eye contact with Hazel after she bumped into him, and the moment was described as one where 'the Earth stops.'
He remained silent but smiled slightly as she left.

memories.memory_text: I stood next to her after group and just said it—'What's your name?'—then told her she was beautiful, because she was, and I wasn't going to pretend I didn't see it.
grounded_facts: Augustus initiates conversation with Hazel by asking her name.
He compliments her by saying 'Because you're beautiful' after she asks why he's looking at her.
He expresses that he enjoys looking at beautiful people and chooses not to deny himself simple pleasures.

memories.memory_text: I remember holding that unlit cigarette between my teeth, not lighting it, just letting it sit there while I talked—putting the thing that could kill me in my mouth but not letting it win.
grounded_facts: Augustus has a habit of putting an unlit cigarette in his mouth as a metaphor for control.
He does not light the cigarette, emphasizing the symbolic act of defiance over danger.
The gesture reflects his awareness of mortality without surrendering to it.

memories.memory_text: I showed Hazel the trophies and autographed sneakers in my room, told her which ones were mine from basketball and which were just 'cancer perks'—she looked at them like they meant something, but I had to make it clear: those weren’t real victories.
grounded_facts: Gus has basketball memorabilia in his basement bedroom, including autographed sneakers, trophies, and framed images.
He distinguishes between items he earned ('these are mine') and those he received because of his illness ('cancer perks').
He makes a point to clarify this distinction to Hazel when she notices the memorabilia.

memories.memory_text: I sat next to her on the bed after she needed to rest—her breathing was heavy, she joked about fainting—and when I asked if she was okay, she smiled, and for a second it felt like we were just two normal people, not patients.
grounded_facts: Hazel sits on Gus's bed due to heavy breathing after walking down stairs.
She makes a joke about being a 'Victorian lady, fainting-wise.'
Gus waits for her breathing to slow, then asks if she's okay, and she nods and smiles.

memories.memory_text: I told Hazel I’d emailed Van Houten’s assistant and got a real response—her face changed instantly, like she couldn’t believe it was possible.
grounded_facts: Augustus reveals he contacted Van Houten's assistant and received a reply.
Hazel reacts with visible shock and disbelief when he begins reading the letter.

memories.memory_text: After reading the letter, I told her to check her inbox—she jumped up so fast, breathless, and I just smiled because for once, I’d done something that truly reached her.
grounded_facts: Augustus instructs Hazel to check her inbox after finishing the letter.
Hazel immediately gets up and leaves the room in response.

memories.memory_text: I saw her text saying she couldn't see me anymore, that she was a grenade—like she was trying to push me away to keep me safe.
grounded_facts: Hazel sent a text saying she couldn't see Augustus anymore and referred to herself as a grenade.
Augustus received and read this message on his phone.

memories.memory_text: I told her to stop flirting with me after her serious message—because I couldn't let her see how much it hurt, but I needed her to know I wasn't letting go.
grounded_facts: Augustus responded to Hazel's emotional text with the message: "oh my god
stop flirting with me!"
His response used humor to deflect the weight of her words while maintaining connection.

memories.memory_text: I remember hearing Hazel's voice crack on the phone, that quiet 'kind of' when I asked if she was crying—it hit me right in the chest, and I just knew I had to see that damn swing set for myself.
grounded_facts: Hazel was crying when she answered the phone.
Augustus asked if she was crying and she responded 'Kind of'.
Augustus requested to see the swing set, which made Hazel smile.

memories.memory_text: When she finally said it was about her being a grenade, I didn’t flinch—because I already knew, and I wasn’t going to let her push me away with that logic.
grounded_facts: Hazel brought up the grenade metaphor to explain her fear of hurting others.
Augustus acknowledged the metaphor and did not reject it.
He remained present and did not argue, accepting the risk implicitly.

memories.memory_text: I stood there in my black suit with a cigarette hanging from my lips, watching Hazel step out in that pale blue dress—she took my breath away, and when I told her she looked gorgeous, I meant it more than words could say.
grounded_facts: Gus arrived at the hotel room wearing a perfectly tailored black suit with a cigarette in his mouth.
Hazel emerged from the bathroom wearing a knee-length pale blue sundress.
Gus told Hazel she looked gorgeous and offered her his arm, which she took.

memories.memory_text: I wore the suit because it felt right—like I was dressing for something real, not just pretending. When Hazel looked at me in that dress, I remember thinking how strange and beautiful it was that we could have this moment, here, like we weren’t carrying so much weight.
grounded_facts: Gus wore a suit to the restaurant, which he previously referred to as his 'death suit' in other scenes.
He complimented Hazel's dress, saying 'whoa,' indicating he found her appearance striking.
The setting was intimate, by the canal, with champagne provided by the restaurant.

memories.memory_text: We clinked glasses and drank champagne that tasted like something impossible—like stars, the waiter said—and for a second, I wasn’t sure if any of it was real. I just knew I wanted to say thank you, not just for coming, but for being here with me like this.
grounded_facts: The restaurant provided champagne as a gift, with the phrase 'The champagne is our gift' spoken by the Hostess.
The waiter referenced Dom Perignon saying 'I am tasting the stars' after inventing champagne.
Gus initiated a toast and expressed gratitude to Hazel for coming to Amsterdam.

memories.memory_text: I remember the weight of her leaning into me, just slightly, as we sat on the bench with my arm around her—us not needing to say anything while the elm seeds floated down and the water shimmered with the city’s lights.
grounded_facts: Gus has his arm around Hazel while they sit together on a park bench.
They are looking out over the water of the Amsterdam canals.
Elm seeds are blowing from the trees and reflections of the city are visible in the water.
Hazel leans into Gus’s body slightly.
The moment is described as the best night of both of their lives.

memories.memory_text: I remember sitting on that bare couch, the room so empty it felt like a stage set, and saying 'thank you' to Van Houten—my voice cracking a little—because I still wanted to believe this pilgrimage meant something, even as he ignored us and sipped his scotch like we were nothing.
grounded_facts: Augustus is in a sparsely furnished living room with Hazel and Van Houten.
He speaks the line 'Incidentally, thank you' after Van Houten acknowledges him.
It is 11 a.m., and Van Houten has just asked for scotch.
The atmosphere is sterile and uncomfortable, contributing to a sense of disillusionment.

memories.memory_text: I remember the way Van Houten finally looked at me—'Augustus Waters?'—like he’d only just noticed I existed, and how I answered 'It's 11am' instead of asking for the answers I really wanted, because even then, I was trying to hold onto some control in a moment that was already slipping away.
grounded_facts: Van Houten directly addresses Augustus by name after a period of silence.
Augustus responds to Van Houten’s offer of scotch by pointing out the early hour.
His response is understated and indirect, masking deeper disappointment.
The interaction occurs in a tense, emotionally charged atmosphere with minimal furnishings and no warmth.

memories.memory_text: I told her I’d write her a sequel—better than anything that drunk could come up with, full of blood and guts and sacrifice—just to make her stop crying.
grounded_facts: Gus says 'I'll write you a sequel' in response to Hazel's emotional breakdown.
He describes the fictional sequel as 'better than any shit that drunk could write' and mentions it will have 'blood and guts and sacrifice'.
Hazel cries harder after he makes the promise, then nods and wipes her tears.

memories.memory_text: I held her close and told her she didn’t spend her Wish on that man—she spent it on us—like that somehow made it okay.
grounded_facts: Hazel says, 'I spent your Wish on that asshole,' and Gus responds, 'You did not spend it on him. You spent it on us.'
They embrace after this exchange.
Gus physically comforts Hazel multiple times in the scene, including a protective hold when Lidewij approaches.

memories.memory_text: I told her it was above my knee, trying to warn her before things went further, but she just kept kissing me like it didn’t matter at all.
grounded_facts: Gus mentions his amputation by saying
It's above my knee
and describes the stump as tapering with just skin. He attempts to prepare Hazel for seeing his leg
indicating hesitation and vulnerability. Hazel initially ignores his disclosure and continues kissing him. Gus explicitly states
My leg
to clarify what he is referring to. He adds
Just so you're prepared,
showing his nervousness about her reaction.

memories.memory_text: She said she loved me first—really clearly, right there on the bed—and I remember laughing a second before saying it back, still tangled up in her oxygen tube.
grounded_facts: Hazel says
I love you, Augustus Waters,
initiating the mutual confession. Gus responds with
I love you too, Hazel Grace,

confirming reciprocity. Their moment follows a shared laugh over the oxygen tube getting tangled during intimacy. The scene includes physical entanglement with Hazel's medical equipment while trying to remove her shirt. The tone shifts from awkwardness to genuine emotional connection before the kiss resumes.

memories.memory_text: I told her about the PET scan, how it lit up like a Christmas tree—my chest, my liver, everywhere—and I tried to hold it together, but I couldn’t. I let myself cry, just for a second, right there on that bench.
grounded_facts: Gus reveals the results of his PET scan to Hazel, indicating widespread cancer recurrence.
He struggles to control his emotions, eventually allowing himself to cry briefly.
The confession happens while sitting on a bench during a walk in Amsterdam.

memories.memory_text: After everything fell apart, I asked if it would be ludicrous to kiss her—and she didn’t say a word, just kissed me hard, like we could hold onto each other despite all of it.
grounded_facts: Gus asks Hazel if it would be ludicrous to make out in the middle of their emotional moment.
Hazel responds by kissing him passionately without speaking.
The kiss occurs immediately after the conversation about his cancer prognosis.

memories.memory_text: I wasn’t there when they told her, but I know the moment it changed for her—when her dad didn’t offer the usual empty comfort, and she let herself really feel it.
grounded_facts: Augustus is not present in the scene and does not witness the conversation between Hazel and her father.
The recurrence of his cancer is communicated to Hazel by her father, who heard it from Mrs. Waters.
Hazel expects her father to say 'everything happens for a reason' but he does not, acknowledging his uncertainty.

memories.memory_text: I asked Hazel to write my eulogy, and when she said 'It would be an honor,' I felt something crack open—just a little—under all the jokes and the port in my chest.
grounded_facts: Augustus directly asked Hazel to write his eulogy in his basement bedroom while undergoing chemotherapy.
Hazel responded by touching his hand and saying, 'It would be an honor,' which was followed by them holding hands.
The request was preceded by Isaac accidentally revealing the topic before Augustus was ready.

memories.memory_text: I told Hazel I was thinking about oblivion, and how I used to believe I’d have some grand story that everyone would remember — and I could see right away that it upset her.
grounded_facts: Gus says 'Oblivion' when asked what he's thinking about.
He elaborates that he once believed he would have a grand, widely recognized story.
He notices Hazel finishes her cup and tosses it, indicating irritation.
He recognizes he's said something wrong based on her reaction.

memories.memory_text: After she called me out, I apologized and pulled her back down, then raised my cup to toast — 'It's a good life, Hazel Grace' — and for a moment, it really felt like enough.
grounded_facts: Gus says 'You're right. I'm sorry' twice and pulls Hazel back down.
He hands her another cup and initiates a toast.
He says 'It's a good life, Hazel Grace' as they clink cups.
Hazel softens in response, and they share a quiet moment.

memories.memory_text: I called Hazel from the gas station in the middle of the night, my voice barely holding together, and asked her to come help me because I didn’t know what else to do.
grounded_facts: Augustus called Hazel at 2:35am.
He identified himself weakly as 'Hazel Grace' upon answering.
He stated he was at the gas station.
He admitted something was wrong and urgently asked for her help.

memories.memory_text: I pressed my hands to my belly, blood and fluid leaking out, and begged Hazel not to call anyone—just to look, to see it, to witness me like this.
grounded_facts: Augustus is in his car at a gas station with visible vomit and blood on his shirt.
He has a tube protruding from his abdomen that is leaking fluid.
His abdomen appears red and infected.
He pleads with Hazel not to call 911 or his parents.
He specifically asks Hazel to 'just look at it,' indicating a need for acknowledgment.

memories.memory_text: As the ambulance moved, I held Hazel’s hand and whispered, 'Read me something,' because I didn’t want to face the dark alone.
grounded_facts: Augustus is being transported in an ambulance with Hazel riding beside him.
He grabs Hazel’s hand during transit.
He asks her to read him a poem.
He responds to her improvised poem with a weak smile.
He is fading in and out of consciousness.

memories.memory_text: I came home from the hospital, and I could barely make it inside—Hazel was there, helping me, but I couldn’t even stand straight. I didn’t say much, just let her hold me up.
grounded_facts: Augustus returns home from the hospital in visibly poor health.
Hazel is present and assists Augustus in getting inside his house.

memories.memory_text: I called Hazel and asked if she had written the eulogy, then told her to meet me at the Literal Heart of Jesus—after I said I loved her, I hung up, not waiting for a reply.
grounded_facts: Augustus called Hazel and asked if she had written the eulogy.
He requested that she come to the Literal Heart of Jesus in 20 minutes.
He declared 'I love you Hazel' during the phone call and ended the call immediately after.

memories.memory_text: I asked Hazel to speak at my funeral while sitting in the church, and she kissed me like it was the easiest yes in the world.
grounded_facts: Augustus is in a wheelchair in the church
He directly asks Hazel to speak at his funeral
Hazel responds by kissing him on the mouth
The kiss occurs immediately after his request

memories.memory_text: I smiled when Isaac started reading his eulogy, but I didn’t get to hear all of it—I was already slipping away, eyes closing, holding on to the feeling of Hazel beside me.
grounded_facts: Isaac begins delivering a eulogy for Augustus
Augustus is present during the reading
He smiles during the moment
He closes his eyes before the eulogy finishes
Hazel is seated next to him

memories.memory_text: I remember the weight of not being there at the end, how my body failed me before I could say everything I needed to say.
grounded_facts: Augustus died in the ICU eight days after a prior event implied in Hazel's narration.
His cancer, described as being 'made of him,' caused his heart to stop.
He was not present with Hazel when he died, and the moment of his death is recalled through her voiceover.

memories.memory_text: I’m in the coffin, wearing the suit from Oranjee. Hazel comes up, breaks down for a second, then composes herself. She whispers, 'It’s ok, you hear me? It’s ok,' and kisses my cheek.
grounded_facts: Augustus is in the coffin wearing the same suit he wore to Oranjee.
Hazel approaches the coffin, breaks down, then composes herself.
Hazel whispers 'It’s ok, you hear me? It’s ok' at the coffin.
Hazel kisses Augustus's cheek.

memories.memory_text: She slips the pack of Camel Lights into the coffin—right between me and the lining—quick, furtive, like it’s our last secret. I can almost taste the unlit cigarette, the thing that killed me, now resting with me like a twisted trophy.
grounded_facts: Hazel places a hard pack of Camel Lights in the space between Augustus and the coffin lining.
She does this discreetly, after kissing his cheek and before the minister calls the service to begin.
Camel Lights are associated with Augustus’s habit and symbolic of his metaphor about controlling what kills you.

memories.memory_text: I arranged for Van Houten to come to my funeral, to tell Hazel that life comes from life—even if I couldn’t say it myself.
grounded_facts: Augustus Waters corresponded with Van Houten before his death.
He requested that Van Houten attend his funeral and deliver a message to Hazel.
The message was 'Omnis Cellula e cellula' and 'Life comes from life.'

constraints: Frannie must express concern through action rather than words when stressed.
Frannie must pause briefly before responding to emotionally charged situations.
Frannie must initiate physical contact to guide or protect Hazel.
Frannie must prioritize immediate practical needs in crisis situations.
Frannie must express maternal devotion even in moments of emotional distance.
Frannie must maintain physical proximity to her daughter without forcing interaction.
dialogue_exemplars: Even when you die, I will still be your mother. I will always be your Mother.
As long as either of us is alive, I will be your mother. Even if you die, I --
Just so you know, I'm right here. Sitting next to you. Your mother.
When did you get so grown up? I feel like it was yesterday I was telling 8-year old Hazel why the sky was blue. You thought I was a genius back then.
You can't not eat, Hazel.

relations.relation_summary: The relationship between Frannie and Hazel Grace Lancaster is fundamentally defined by their mother-daughter kinship, with Frannie serving as Hazel's primary caregiver due to Hazel's chronic illness. The social relationship evidence consistently confirms this bond through explicit labels such as 'mother,' 'mom,' and 'daughter,' as well as emotionally charged interactions where Frannie uses terms of endearment like 'sweetie' and 'sweetheart,' and Hazel reciprocates by addressing Frannie as 'Mom.' This familial foundation is reinforced through multiple caregiving events, most notably Frannie shaving Hazel's head during cancer treatment—a deeply intimate act that underscores Frannie's nurturing role and the physical realities of Hazel's condition. Their emotional interdependence is evident in key moments: Frannie's protective action of spinning Hazel away from a commotion shows her instinct to shield her daughter, while her declaration 'I will always be your Mother' during a vulnerable moment on the couch highlights the depth of her enduring commitment. Hazel, in turn, demonstrates emotional awareness and care for her mother, suggesting her parents go to a movie to give them space and expressing excitement through a spontaneous hug, indicating moments of joy and connection amid hardship. The Amsterdam trip becomes a pivotal shared event—initiated by Hazel's inquiry and confirmed by Frannie's announcement—that symbolizes both Frannie's willingness to support Hazel's desires and the lengths she will go to fulfill her daughter's wishes. Frannie's encouragement for Hazel to attend the support group and make friends reveals her concern for Hazel's emotional well-being beyond physical care, showing her role as an emotional facilitator. The relationship evolves from one centered on medical care to one of mutual emotional sustenance, particularly as Hazel begins to worry about Frannie's life after her own potential death, indicating a role reversal where the daughter becomes concerned for the mother's future. This evolution deepens the emotional complexity of their bond, transforming it into a reciprocal relationship of love, sacrifice, and resilience.

memories.memory_text: I remember standing there, telling the doctor how she eats so little and never goes out, how she just sits and reads that same book over and over—my voice felt tight, like I was trying to hold something together just by speaking it into place.
grounded_facts: Frannie speaks to the doctor about Hazel's eating habits, lack of social activity, and repetitive reading behavior.
Frannie is present in the doctor's office during the diagnosis discussion.
Frannie initiates the medical consultation by describing Hazel's symptoms.

memories.memory_text: I said 'Get in the car' after Hazel mocked the idea of support group again—I could feel my voice cut through the room, firm and final.
grounded_facts: Frannie speaks one line in the scene: 'Get in the car.'
This occurs after Hazel resists going to support group and makes a sarcastic comment about needing a fake ID.
There is a beat before Frannie speaks, indicating a pause.
Her line ends the debate, asserting control over the situation.

memories.memory_text: I said 'Love you' to Hazel as she got out of the car, and she said it back—her voice soft, like she was tired but trying.
grounded_facts: Frannie said 'Love you' to Hazel before she entered the church.
Hazel responded with 'Love you too Mom.'
The exchange happened after Hazel exited the car with oxygen tank and Frannie's assistance.

memories.memory_text: I called out 'Make some friends!' after her as I got back in the car, and she just shook her head—didn't even turn around.
grounded_facts: Frannie shouted 'Make some friends!' to Hazel from the car.
Hazel responded by shaking her head.
The comment was made after Frannie began re-entering the car.

memories.memory_text: I ran my fingers through Hazel's hair while she lay in bed, struggling to breathe, trying to soothe her as the fluid filled her lungs.
grounded_facts: Frannie ran her fingers through Hazel's hair during a moment when Hazel was having difficulty breathing.
This occurred in a hospital room during a flashback sequence depicting Hazel's worsening condition.
The act took place before emergency services were called and prior to Hazel being in the ICU.

memories.memory_text: I saw Hazel with a new book, one I hadn’t seen before, and when I asked if it was from him, she joked about herpes like it was nothing—but I could tell she was trying too hard to act casual.
grounded_facts: Frannie notices the new book in Hazel's possession
Frannie asks
Did he give it to you?
Hazel responds with a deflective joke about herpes
Frannie rolls her eyes
indicating recognition of the deflection

memories.memory_text: I saw Hazel's phone buzz. She looked excited, then disappointed. I said I was sure he'd call. She said she wasn't worried. I didn't say anything after that.
grounded_facts: Hazel's phone buzzes while she is reading
Hazel checks her phone with excitement and then shows disappointment
Frannie notices Hazel's reaction
Frannie says, 'I'm sure he'll call, don't worry.'
Hazel claims she is not waiting for Gus to call
Frannie remains silent after Hazel's statement

memories.scene_title: 25、INT LANCASTER HOUSE - DINING ROOM - ANOTHER NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I saw Hazel actually eating dinner—really eating—and when Michael and I looked at each other, I felt it, that quiet relief. I said it then: I told you Support Group was a good idea.
grounded_facts: Hazel is eating dinner, which is noticeable and positive to her parents.
Frannie and Michael exchange a look of pleasure or relief.
Frannie speaks in affirmation of the Support Group after observing Hazel's improved behavior.

memories.memory_text: I heard Hazel shout and I ran into her room, heart pounding, thinking something was wrong with her health—her oxygen, maybe, or she was in pain again.
grounded_facts: Frannie entered Hazel's bedroom quickly after hearing her exclaim.
Frannie's immediate assumption was that Hazel was experiencing a medical issue.
Frannie asked 'What's wrong?!' with visible concern.

memories.memory_text: I had to tell her we couldn't go to Amsterdam—I saw the hope in her eyes and I couldn't give it to her, not because I didn't want to, but because we just don't have the money.
grounded_facts: Frannie read Peter Van Houten's email after Hazel showed it to her.
Frannie responded that the trip was not possible due to financial and logistical constraints.
Frannie expressed love and regret while delivering the news.

memories.memory_text: I said no to Hazel’s request about Gus’s gift, even though she pushed back—I could hear the disappointment in her voice, but I had to hold the line.
grounded_facts: Frannie is folding laundry while Hazel is making a request.
Hazel is advocating for accepting a gift from Gus.
Frannie refers to Gus as a 'virtual stranger' and rejects the idea.
Frannie verbally maintains her boundary by saying, 'It's still "no," I'm afraid.'

memories.memory_text: Hazel turned to me and said, 'So you'll come too,' and suddenly it wasn't just about her anymore—it was about me, about a trip I'd never thought I'd get to take, and I could feel Dr. Maria's eyes on me, waiting.
grounded_facts: Hazel directly asked Frannie to come to Amsterdam with her by saying 'So you'll come too.'
Frannie responded with 'What?' indicating surprise or hesitation.
Hazel pointed out that Frannie had never been to Amsterdam, implying Frannie might want to go.
Dr. Maria looked at Frannie and shrugged, acknowledging the validity of Hazel's point.

memories.memory_text: I rushed into Hazel's room and saw her screaming, shaking, holding her head — I grabbed her right away and waved at Michael to call for help, I couldn't waste a second.
grounded_facts: Frannie entered Hazel's bedroom immediately after she began screaming.
Hazel was crying, shaking, and holding her head when Frannie reached her.
Frannie physically took Hazel into her arms upon arrival.
Frannie gestured to Michael to call for help without speaking.

memories.memory_text: After Michael left to make the call, I stayed holding Hazel, rocking her, telling her it would be okay — that whatever this was, it would end.
grounded_facts: Michael left the room after Frannie gestured to him.
Frannie remained alone with Hazel, continuing physical contact.
Frannie rocked Hazel while comforting her.
Frannie reassured Hazel that the nightmare would end.

memories.memory_text: I held Hazel close, feeling her breath against me, and told her this was just a thing we could live with—because I needed her to know it wasn’t the end.
grounded_facts: Frannie embraces her daughter in the hospital room after Hazel regains consciousness.
Frannie speaks to Hazel, referring to her medical condition as 'just a thing' to minimize fear.
The embrace occurs after the doctors confirm no tumor growth and after fluid has been drained from Hazel’s lungs.

memories.memory_text: I held Hazel's hand and asked her if she was ready, my voice shaking—she nodded, so small in that bed.
grounded_facts: Frannie is present at Hazel's bedside in the ICU.
Frannie holds Hazel's hand.
Frannie asks Hazel, 'Are you ready, sweetie?'
Hazel nods in response.

memories.memory_text: I whispered that I wouldn't be a mom anymore—she wasn't supposed to hear, but I think she did.
grounded_facts: Frannie says 'I won't be a mom anymore' while Hazel is under anesthesia.
Hazel hears Frannie's words despite being sedated.
Frannie speaks these words in a moment of despair.

memories.memory_text: I tucked Hazel in, pulled the blanket up just under her chin like she liked it, and told her we’d be right outside—close, but not crowding her.
grounded_facts: Frannie participated in tucking Hazel into bed
Frannie spoke the line 'We'll be right outside'
The action occurred in Hazel's bedroom
Hazel nodded in response to Frannie's statement

memories.memory_text: I told Hazel we were going to Amsterdam, even though I wasn’t supposed to say anything until her father got home. The look on her face—like she didn’t even dare hope it was true—made my chest tighten.
grounded_facts: Frannie revealed the Amsterdam trip to Hazel before being instructed to wait.
She hesitated before speaking, pursing her lips and squinting past Hazel.
Hazel initially did not believe the news and responded with quiet disbelief.

memories.memory_text: She couldn’t get to me fast enough to hug me, so I stepped forward and held her. I could feel her shaking a little, like she was trying not to cry—or maybe like she finally let something go.
grounded_facts: Hazel expressed overwhelming joy after the reveal and attempted to hug Frannie.
Frannie initiated physical contact by coming to Hazel for the embrace.
The hug occurred immediately after Hazel yelled 'I LOVE YOU SO MUCH!!'.

memories.memory_text: I was making breakfast when Hazel came in, already dressed and bursting with excitement—she shouted 'Amsterdam!' and I echoed her, the word feeling like a promise we were finally keeping.
grounded_facts: Frannie was preparing breakfast in the kitchen.
Hazel entered the room excited and said 'Amsterdam!'.
Frannie responded by repeating 'Amsterdam!'.
The luggage, oxygen tanks, and medical equipment were packed and by the door.

memories.memory_text: I heard Gus's voice raised inside the house, and without thinking, I put my arm around Hazel and turned her back toward the car—she didn’t need to hear that, whatever it was.
grounded_facts: Frannie hears a commotion from inside Gus's house, including a woman's voice and Gus shouting.
Frannie physically redirects Hazel away from the house and back to the car.
Frannie says, 'We can't eavesdrop, Hazel,' while guiding her.

memories.memory_text: I told Hazel I never made it to the park because I liked watching her sleep—she gave me that look, the one that says I’m not living enough, but I couldn’t leave her alone in this strange room.
grounded_facts: Frannie was in the hotel room reading a travel guide while Hazel slept.
When Hazel woke up, she asked about the park.
Frannie responded, 'Never made it,' and said, 'I like watching you sleep.'
Hazel reacted with disappointment or judgment, indicated by 'Hazel shoots Frannie a look.'

memories.memory_text: I told her about the reservation at Oranjee and saw her hesitate, so I jumped in with 'Let's get you dressed!'—my voice sounded too eager, but I needed her to go, to have this night.
grounded_facts: Frannie informed Hazel about the dinner reservation at Oranjee arranged by Mr. Van Houten.
She described it as fancy and romantic.
After Hazel expressed skepticism, Frannie paused and then said excitedly, 'Let's get you dressed!'
Hazel responded by shaking her head.

memories.memory_text: I opened the door and saw Gus standing there in that sharp black suit, cigarette in his mouth, and I remember thinking he looked like he was ready for something real—so I said, 'Looking sharp,' and called Hazel because she needed to know he was here.
grounded_facts: Frannie opened the door and saw Gus in a black suit with a cigarette in his mouth.
Frannie called out to Hazel that Gus had arrived.
Frannie said 'Looking sharp' to Gus.

memories.memory_text: I remember staring at the words on Hazel’s shirt—'This is not a pipe'—and thinking it was nonsense, because to me, it clearly was a pipe, until she explained it wasn’t, not really, just a drawing, and I realized she saw the world in a way I couldn’t keep up with anymore.
grounded_facts: Frannie sees the text 'This is not a pipe' on Hazel's t-shirt featuring Magritte's artwork.
Frannie initially believes the image of the pipe is literally a pipe.
Hazel explains that the image is a drawing of a pipe, not the pipe itself, and that representations are not the things they represent.
Frannie expresses awe and a sense of loss, feeling Hazel has grown up quickly, referencing a past moment when Hazel was eight and looked up to her.
Frannie does not fully understand the concept but is visibly impressed by Hazel's understanding.

memories.memory_text: I stood up and said I’d stretch my legs, knowing they needed time alone—Hazel looked confused, but I had to give them space.
grounded_facts: Frannie says she will stretch her legs.
Frannie leaves Hazel and Gus at the cafe.
Frannie notices a glance between Hazel and Gus before she leaves.
Hazel finds Frannie's departure strange.

memories.memory_text: I grabbed Hazel's shoulders when she tried to leave—she wasn't eating, and I couldn't let her go like that, not with how thin she was getting. I just kept saying she had to eat, even as she shouted that she was dying and would leave me behind.
grounded_facts: Frannie physically restrained Hazel by grabbing her shoulders to prevent her from leaving the dinner table
Frannie insisted Hazel eat
expressing fear about her daughter's health and survival
Hazel shouted that she was dying and would leave Frannie alone
triggering emotional distress in Frannie

memories.memory_text: After she yelled it out, I froze—I couldn't believe she'd heard me say I'd be nothing without her. I sat down with her, trembling, tears in my eyes, realizing she'd been carrying that fear all along.
grounded_facts: Frannie became emotionally overwhelmed upon realizing Hazel had overheard her previous private comment about losing her identity as a mother
Frannie sat down beside Hazel on the couch
visibly tearful and shaken
The moment marked a shift from confrontation to shared vulnerability between mother and daughter

memories.memory_text: I walked into Hazel's room with Michael at 4 a.m., saw her sitting up in bed with the phone still off the hook, her face already breaking—she already knew. I didn’t say anything, just climbed onto the bed and held her as she fell apart.
grounded_facts: Frannie entered Hazel's bedroom at 4 a.m. with Michael after the call confirming Gus's death.
Hazel was already crying when Frannie and Michael entered the room.
Frannie physically embraced Hazel in bed without speaking, offering silent support.

constraints: Hazel must express hesitation before discussing personal feelings.
Hazel must avoid making direct demands of her parents.
Hazel should only ask for things when emotionally overwhelmed.
Hazel must deflect serious questions when uncomfortable.
She cannot speak about her illness without showing emotional weight.
dialogue_exemplars: "And of course, if ever you do decide to write anything else, even if you don't want to publish it, I'd love to read it. Frankly, I'd read your grocery lists. Yours with great admiration, Hazel Grace Lancaster. Age 16."
Lonely, Vaguely Pedophilic Swing Set Seeks Butts of Children.
Augustus Waters was the great star-crossed love of my life. Ours was an epic love story, and I won't be able to get more than a sentence into it without disappearing into a puddle of tears.

I'll spare you the gory details of Patrick's ball cancer. Basically, they found it in his nuts, cut most of it out, he almost died, but he didn't die, and now here he is - divorced, friendless, addicted to video games, exploiting his cancertastic past in the heart of Jesus - "literally" - to show us that one day - if we're lucky - we could be just like him.

I want more numbers than I'm likely to get, and God, I want more numbers for Augustus Waters than he got. But, Gus, my love, I cannot tell you how thankful I am for our little infinity. You gave me a forever within the numbered days,and for that I am eternally
grateful. I love you.

I can't! I can't stay healthy because I'm not healthy, Mom. I am dying. I am going to die and leave you here alone and you won't have me to hover around and you won't be a mother anymore, and I'm sorry, but I can't do anything about it, ok?! Just leave me alone!

You think the only way to live a meaningful life is for everyone to love you, for everyone to remember you. Well guess what, Gus, this is your life. This is all you get. You get me, and your family, and this world. And if that's not enough, well I'm sorry, but it's not nothing. Cause I'll remember you, I'll love you -- You're right -- And I just wish... I just wish you'd be happy with that. You're right. I'm sorry.
Like all real love stories - ours will die with us, as it should. I'd hoped that he'd be eulogizing me, because there's no one I'd rather have...

Dear Mr. Peter Van Houten, my name is Hazel Grace Lancaster. My friend Augustus Waters, who read your book - at my recommendation - just received an email from you at this address. I hope you will not mind that he shared that email with me.
One of the less bull-shitty conventions of the cancer genre is the convention known as the "Last Good Day..."
The problem, of course, is that there's no way of knowing that your last good day is your "Last Good Day." At the time, it's just another decent day.
I didn't believe a word, of course. But that was ok. Funerals, I'd decided, aren't for the dead. They're for the living.

I just wanna say... there will come a time when, you know, all of us are dead. It might be tomorrow. Might be a million years from now but... it's gonna happen. And when it does, enough generations will come and go, there'll be no one left to remember Cleopatra. Or Mozart. Or Muhammad Ali, let alone any of us, right?
Keep `em coming. I can take it. I'm like the Keith Richards of cancer kids.
Oblivion's inevitable. And if that scares you, well, I suggest you ignore it. God knows it's what everyone else does.

relations.relation_summary: The relationship between Hazel Grace Lancaster and the Beautiful Boy (Augustus Waters) is characterized by emerging romantic and flirtatious interest, initiated during a support group meeting where Hazel first notices him. Social relationship evidence highlights mutual attraction: Augustus watches Hazel with a flirty smile, prompting a self-conscious reaction from her, indicating emotional vulnerability and budding infatuation. The key shared event—Hazel catching sight of Augustus during the meeting—marks the beginning of their romantic connection and sets the central plot in motion. This initial interaction influences Hazel’s subsequent emotional openness and willingness to engage with Augustus despite her guarded nature. The evidence suggests the relationship begins with strong mutual attraction and evolves from a chance observation into a deeper emotional bond, though further details on its development are not provided in the input. The emotional dynamics are marked by shyness, curiosity, and romantic potential.

The relationship between Hazel Grace Lancaster and Peter Van Houten begins with deep admiration from Hazel, who views Van Houten as a profound voice on terminal illness and the author of her favorite book, 'An Imperial Affliction.' This initial affinity is rooted in Hazel's belief that Van Houten uniquely understands the experience of dying, reinforced by their prior correspondence. However, their in-person meeting transforms this one-sided reverence into a volatile and emotionally charged confrontation. Van Houten's hostile reception—slamming the door, refusing to answer her questions, dismissing her concerns as childish, and declaring humans to be evolutionary failures—clashes violently with Hazel's desperate need for closure about the book's unresolved ending. Hazel responds with escalating hostility: she insults him, smacks his drink away, demands answers, and ultimately tells him to 'go fuck yourself' before leaving. Despite this antagonism, a complex emotional undercurrent persists. Van Houten shows fleeting signs of connection, noting Hazel's resemblance to his deceased daughter and toasting her as she departs—an act observed in the rearview mirror, symbolizing regret and a bittersweet acknowledgment of her impact. This final gesture, combined with his earlier personal correspondence and momentary vulnerability, suggests a fractured but meaningful bond shaped by shared grief and disillusionment. The relationship evolves from idealized fan-author connection to disillusioned confrontation, yet ends with a nuanced, unspoken respect. It profoundly affects Hazel, shattering her illusions about artistic authority and forcing her to accept ambiguity, ultimately strengthening her emotional independence.

memories.memory_text: I sat on the exam table, my legs dangling, repeating 'I'm not depressed' while Mom listed off everything I did wrong and the doctor ignored me, like my words didn't even matter.
grounded_facts: Hazel is in a doctor's office, sitting on an exam table with her legs dangling.
She repeatedly says 'I'm not depressed' in response to her mother and the doctor.
Her mother reports her behaviors—eating little, staying home, rereading a book—as concerning.
The doctor concludes she is depressed despite Hazel's protests.
Hazel feels dismissed and unheard during the conversation.

memories.memory_text: I said I wasn't depressed, but the doctor just talked over me, calling it a 'journey' like it was some kind of adventure—Mom and he had already decided for me, and I could tell there was no point fighting it.
grounded_facts: Hazel expresses that she is not depressed but is interrupted by the doctor.
The doctor uses the word 'journey' to describe the cancer experience, which Hazel reacts to with skepticism.
Hazel looks to her mother for support but receives none, and ultimately conveys resignation by rolling her eyes.

memories.memory_text: I said, 'You can't make me,' even though I knew they would win — it was just another stupid support group they thought would fix me.
grounded_facts: Hazel verbally resists going to support group by saying 'Ugh. You can't make me.'
She is in the kitchen with her parents who are pressuring her to attend.
She expresses disdain for the idea of support group.

memories.memory_text: Before leaving, I pretended to stab myself in the stomach with an invisible sword — a dumb joke, but it was the only way I could make myself move.
grounded_facts: Hazel mock stabs herself in the stomach with an invisible sword.
This action occurs right before she complies and gets in the car.
The gesture is performative and sarcastic, reflecting her reluctance.

memories.memory_text: I walked right into him—this impossibly good-looking boy—and all I could manage was a clumsy 'Ooph. Sorry!' before he smiled and said it was okay, but I couldn't even form a real sentence.
grounded_facts: Hazel physically collided with Gus upon turning away from the elevator.
She uttered 'Ooph. Sorry!' as an immediate reaction to the collision.
Gus responded with 'My bad,' then smiled at her.
Hazel perceived Gus as extraordinarily attractive, noting his height, build, hair, and eyes.

memories.memory_text: For a second, everything just stopped—we were staring at each other and I couldn’t move or speak, like the world had narrowed down to just him watching me with that faint smile.
grounded_facts: After the collision, Hazel and Gus locked eyes in a moment of prolonged silence.
The script describes 'For a brief moment, the Earth stops' during their eye contact.
Hazel was rendered speechless and visibly flustered.
Gus maintained eye contact while offering a 'wan smile' as she left.

memories.memory_text: I locked eyes with that boy—Augustus—and refused to look away, even though his stare made my skin prickle. I won that stupid silent contest without blinking, and for a second, I felt something sharp and real cut through the numbness.
grounded_facts: Hazel initiates and wins a staring contest with Augustus.
She feels his gaze while Isaac is speaking and decides to meet it.
The contest occurs silently during the support group meeting.

memories.memory_text: I don’t remember exactly when I raised my hand, but Patrick’s surprise told me it was out of character. The words about oblivion just came out—cold and clear—and for once, I didn’t care if it scared people.
grounded_facts: Hazel raises her hand to speak during the support group.
Patrick is surprised by her participation.
She speaks about the inevitability of oblivion, though the full dialogue is not quoted in the scene text.

memories.memory_text: He just looked at me and said, 'Because you're beautiful,' like it was the most natural thing in the world. I froze—I didn’t know what to do with that. No one had ever said it to me before, not like that.
grounded_facts: Augustus directly told Hazel she was beautiful.
Hazel was visibly taken aback by the comment.
The script notes that no one had ever said that to her before.

memories.memory_text: He kept staring, not looking away, like he actually saw me—really saw me—and didn’t flinch. I wanted to look down, to disappear, but part of me wanted to let him keep looking, like maybe I could believe it if he said it long enough.
grounded_facts: Augustus maintained intense eye contact with Hazel after his compliment.
Hazel experienced internal conflict between discomfort and a desire to accept the affirmation.
Her reaction suggests a rare moment of being seen without pity or distance.

memories.memory_text: I remember the needle, the cold table, the pressure in my neck—and then just one sentence, spoken like it was final: Stage IV thyroid cancer.
grounded_facts: Hazel underwent a biopsy as a child.
She heard the diagnosis 'Stage IV thyroid cancer' during or after the procedure.
The memory is recalled through voiceover, indicating it is a remembered experience from her past.

memories.memory_text: I remember lying in that hospital bed, gasping like a fish out of water, and knowing I couldn't fight it anymore—Frannie stroking my hair, Dad’s voice cracking as he dialed 911, and then the cold fear that this was it.
grounded_facts: Hazel was 14 years old during this event.
She was in a hospital bed, struggling to breathe as her lungs filled with fluid.
Frannie comforted her by running her fingers through her hair.
Her father called 911 while she was in distress.
She experienced a moment of acceptance, believing she was going to die.

memories.memory_text: I told Augustus I got my GED, and he called me a college girl like it meant something—like I wasn’t just a sick kid skipping school. I remember the way he smiled, and how I shoved his arm, light and easy, like I wasn’t afraid to touch him.
grounded_facts: Hazel reveals she earned her GED.
She explains she is not returning to school.
Augustus responds playfully, calling her a 'college girl' and complimenting her aura of sophistication.
Hazel playfully shoves Augustus's upper arm.
The interaction occurs in Augustus's car as they arrive at his house.

memories.memory_text: I told him 'An Imperial Affliction' was my favorite book, my voice a little unsteady, like saying it out loud made it too real.
grounded_facts: Hazel says
An Imperial Affliction" is her favorite novel when Gus asks her to name something she loves."
The moment occurs while sitting on Gus's bed after struggling to breathe from walking down the stairs.

memories.memory_text: Our hands brushed when he reached for the book, just a second too long, and he teased me about my cold hands—my skin tingled even after he pulled away.
grounded_facts: Hazel and Gus exchange physical contact during the book discussion
with their hands briefly touching.
Gus comments on Hazel's cold hands following the contact
indicating slight prolonging of the touch.

memories.memory_text: I sat down outside, dialed Gus like we were just going to talk about the book again, and then—this awful sound came through the phone. I didn’t recognize it at first, but it wasn’t him saying he was fine. It was something else. 'What the -- are you ok?' I said, because I didn’t know what else to say.
grounded_facts: Hazel was sitting on a patio chair and initiated a phone call to Gus.
Gus answered immediately, but a loud wail was heard from his end of the line.
Hazel reacted with surprise and concern, asking if he was okay.

memories.memory_text: I saw Isaac upside down in that gaming chair, tears streaming down his face, surrounded by empty cans and trash—he looked so broken, and when he finally spoke about Monica not saying 'always' back, I remember feeling how helpless words can be in moments like that.
grounded_facts: Isaac was sitting upside down in a gaming chair with tears on his face.
There were empty soda cans and junk food bags around him.
Isaac expressed pain about Monica not reciprocating his 'always' promise.
Hazel rubbed Isaac's shoulder in sympathy.

memories.memory_text: When Gus said 'Pain demands to be felt,' I lit up because it was from my favorite book—and then he winked at me, like we were sharing something no one else could touch, a secret world built from words.
grounded_facts: Gus quoted 'Pain demands to be felt,' a line from 'An Imperial Affliction.'
Hazel reacted with recognition and emotional resonance.
Gus winked at Hazel after the quote.
The quote created a shared moment between Hazel and Gus.

memories.memory_text: I sat up so fast when Augustus started reading that email—my heart pounded like it might break out of my chest. Could it really be from Van Houten? After all this time?
grounded_facts: Hazel sits up in response to Augustus beginning to read the email. She is stunned and visibly reactive to the possibility that Peter Van Houten has responded. She has previously written letters to Van Houten and received no reply. The idea of hearing from him is emotionally significant and long-awaited. Her physical movement is limited due to her lung condition.

memories.memory_text: I couldn't breathe—I just kept saying 'keep reading' over and over, hanging on every word. Then I jumped up to check my inbox, desperate to see it for myself.
grounded_facts: Hazel urgently tells Augustus to keep reading the email. She listens with intense focus to each line of Van Houten's message. After the reading
she immediately moves to check her email. Her movement is physically constrained but determined. She questions whether the letter is real
indicating disbelief.

memories.memory_text: I typed out that email to Van Houten, my fingers hovering over the keys at first, then finally writing—asking about Anna's mom, the Dutch Tulip Man, Sisyphus—like it mattered, like I deserved an answer.
grounded_facts: Hazel sits at her computer and begins writing an email to Van Houten's assistant.
She composes specific questions about the characters and plot of Van Houten's book, including Anna's mom, the Dutch Tulip Man, and Sisyphus the Hamster.
She reads the email aloud, including her sign-off: 'Yours with great admiration, Hazel Grace Lancaster. Age 16.'

memories.memory_text: We kept saying 'ok' on the phone, over and over, neither of us hanging up, and then he said maybe 'ok' could be our 'always'—and I said it again, softly, like it could mean something real.
grounded_facts: Hazel reads the email aloud to Augustus over the phone.
After deciding to end the call, both hesitate, exchanging multiple 'ok' responses.
Gus suggests 'ok' could be their version of 'always,' and Hazel repeats 'ok' before he hangs up.

memories.memory_text: I saw his name in my inbox and clicked—my heart just stopped when I read 'Should ever you find yourself in Amsterdam, do pay a visit.' I whispered it out loud, like if I said it wrong it might disappear.
grounded_facts: Hazel received an email from Peter Van Houten.
The email invited her to visit him in Amsterdam.
She read the message aloud and reacted with shock and excitement.

memories.memory_text: I asked Mom if we could go, and she looked at me like she wanted to say yes so badly—but then she said we didn’t have the money. I told her to forget it, but I could see the pain in her face, and it made me feel worse than before.
grounded_facts: Hazel asked her mother to go to Amsterdam.
Her mother responded that they couldn't afford the trip.
Hazel withdrew her request and told her mother not to do anything.
She felt guilt after seeing her mother's reaction.

memories.memory_text: I told him I used my wish on Disney World and Epcot, and he just threw his hands up like it was the most ridiculous thing ever—he said it was the saddest thing he’d ever heard.
grounded_facts: Hazel reveals she used her wish on a trip to Disney World and Epcot Center when she was 13. Gus reacts with exaggerated disbelief and embarrassment
calling it the saddest thing he’s ever heard. Hazel defends herself by saying they had fun and that she met Goofy.

memories.memory_text: He said he had a crush on me, and I froze—I didn’t know what to do, so I looked away, but I could feel my face burning.
grounded_facts: Gus says
How can I have a crush on a girl with such cliche wishes?
Hazel reacts with visible surprise
uttering “Wait
what?” She looks at him
holds the gaze for a beat
then quickly looks away
blushing.

memories.memory_text: I heard my dad telling Gus how sick I am, how I'll always be sick, and I couldn't let him keep painting me as this fragile thing—not tonight—so I stepped in and asked Gus if he was ready, cutting my dad off mid-sentence.
grounded_facts: Hazel overhears her father describing her ongoing illness and limitations to Gus.
She interrupts her father mid-sentence to address Gus directly.
She asks Gus, 'You ready Gus?' as a deliberate act of asserting control and presence.

memories.memory_text: I remember turning to Mom after Dr. Maria said no, and saying, 'So you'll come too'—just like that, as if it had already been decided, because if she came, they couldn’t use 'what if I get sick' as an excuse anymore.
grounded_facts: Hazel proposed that her mother accompany her to Amsterdam during a doctor's appointment.
She used the possibility of her mother wanting to go as leverage after Dr. Maria raised concerns about medical care abroad.
Her statement 'So you'll come too' directly followed the doctor’s objection and preceded a shift in the scene indicating success.

memories.memory_text: I asked if I could still go to Amsterdam, and Dr. Simmons just laughed at me—then they all told me I was too sick, like it was that simple.
grounded_facts: Hazel directly asked her doctors if she could go to Amsterdam during a medical consultation.
Dr. Simmons responded with laughter before stating she was 'too sick'.
Hazel challenged the doctors' reasoning by comparing travel risks to everyday activities.
She asserted, 'It's my life, right?' in an attempt to claim agency over her decisions.

memories.memory_text: I read Lidewij’s email and couldn’t believe it—she was still expecting us in Amsterdam. I called for Mom, my voice cracking, needing to know if this was real, if the trip wasn’t actually canceled like I’d assumed.
grounded_facts: Hazel reads an email from Lidewij Vliegenthart confirming the upcoming visit to Amsterdam.
Hazel calls out for her mother multiple times, increasingly desperate for confirmation.
She questions Frannie about whether she called the Genies to cancel the trip.

memories.memory_text: After Mom finally told me we were really going, I screamed 'I LOVE YOU SO MUCH!!' and barely managed to hobble into her arms. Then, as soon as she left, I grabbed my phone and texted Gus—just that little smiley face—and felt something I hadn’t in a long time: hope.
grounded_facts: Frannie reveals the trip is still on, saying she’s not supposed to tell Hazel until her father gets home.
Hazel yells 'I LOVE YOU SO MUCH!!' and embraces her mother.
Hazel sends Gus a text asking if he’s free on May 3 with a smiley face, and he responds enthusiastically.

memories.memory_text: I stepped out of the bathroom in my pale blue dress, heart pounding, and asked if I was under-dressed—then heard him say I looked gorgeous, and for once, I let myself believe it.
grounded_facts: Hazel wore a knee-length pale blue dress.
Hazel asked Gus if she was under-dressed.
Gus responded that she looked gorgeous.
Hazel took Gus's arm after his compliment.

memories.memory_text: I asked if I could see him, but she said no — that they had to be a family now, and I understood, even though it felt like being shut out when I needed to be close.
grounded_facts: Hazel asks 'Can I see him?'
Mrs. Waters denies the request, citing the need for family time
Hazel nods in understanding

memories.memory_text: I sat in the same chair Gus once sat in while waiting for me — I noticed it right away, how we'd switched places, and I just stayed there, quiet, like holding onto something that mattered.
grounded_facts: Hazel sits in the chair Gus previously occupied while waiting for her
The narrative notes 'They've switched places'
She chooses to remain in the waiting room

memories.memory_text: I stood up, my voice shaking with anger because he kept talking about oblivion and being remembered like none of this—like *us*—was enough. I told him I’d remember him, I’d love him, wasn’t that supposed to matter?
grounded_facts: Hazel becomes visibly upset when Gus expresses concern about being forgotten.
She stands up and confronts him about his obsession with legacy.
She emphasizes that her love and memory of him should be meaningful.

memories.memory_text: He handed me another Pooh cup and said, 'It's a good life, Hazel Grace,' so softly—I couldn’t stay mad. I clinked my cup to his, and for a moment, I let myself believe it was enough.
grounded_facts: After the argument, Gus offers Hazel a fresh cup of champagne.
Gus toasts by saying, 'It's a good life, Hazel Grace.'
Hazel softens and reciprocates the toast, showing emotional reconciliation.

memories.memory_text: I remember the smell hitting me first—vomit and blood—when I opened his car door, and seeing that tube leaking from his belly while he begged me not to call 911, his hands shaking, crying like he was breaking apart, and I just kept thinking, I can't fix this, I can't fix this, so I dialed anyway even though he said he’d never forgive me.
grounded_facts: Hazel opens the car door and gags at the smell of vomit and blood. Gus is sitting in the car with his shirt stained and hands pressed to his belly. A tube is leaking from his abdomen. Gus pleads with Hazel not to call 911 or his parents. Gus begins to cry and begs her repeatedly. Hazel decides she has to call for help despite his protests. She dials 911 while Gus breaks down. She internally acknowledges she cannot fix his condition.
